summary: Joel Spolsky shares a curated list of interesting links and media recommendations. He highlights an aviation blog called Enplaned, introduces Reddit as a user-submitted links site, and reviews several podcasts. He recommends TWiT and VentureVoice as the only two podcasts worth regular listening, while criticizing the Gilmore Gang for poor audio quality and Adam Curry's show for being too self-referential about podcasting itself.
key_points: Reddit is introduced as a simple user-submitted links site with a cute alien mascot, notable mainly because its founders appeared in the Aardvark'd movie
Of many podcasts tried, only TWiT and VentureVoice survived Joel's quality filter, suggesting most early podcasts were not worth the time
Joel criticizes Adam Curry's podcast for spending most of its time talking about how great podcasting is rather than demonstrating it through quality content
Production quality matters — Joel dropped the Gilmore Gang solely because of poor audio quality making it inaudible on New York streets
money_quotes: Reddit is a website with a cute alien mascot, featuring user-submitted and user-rated links.
I've subscribed and unsubscribed to a lot of Podcasts trying to keep entertained on the subway, and the bottom line is that there are only two I can tolerate on a regular basis.
TWiT made me understand why Leo Laporte was so beloved on Tech TV, and John C. Dvorak will charm your socks off in his role as the cuddly curmudgeon, channeling Jerry Pournelle.
I had to stop listening to the Gilmore Gang because the sound quality made it inaudible on the streets of New York.
Do they listen to their podcasts after they record them?
I had to stop listening to Adam Curry because all he does is talk about was how great podcasts are. Show us, Adam, don't tell us.
Adam Curry is one of the best DJs in the world, but his show is 10% great DJing, 60% crappy drivel about podcasting, and 30% late-night-infomercial-quality promos for podcasts about garden slugs.
key_insight: Show, don't tell — whether in podcasting or any medium, demonstrating quality through your work is more convincing than talking about how great the medium itself is.
